coredata.dc:title: Inulin-based fluorescent carbon dots as effective probes for rapid/visual detection of 3-nitrotyrosine
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Yaxuan
Wang, Chen
Guo, Yushi
Yang, Wenxiao
Zhang, Guolin
Wu, Qiuhua
coredata.dc:description: Inulin-based nitrogen-doped carbon dots (I-CDs) with a quantum yield of 28.6 % were synthesized by hydrothermal method. I-CDs exhibited a narrow particle size distribution, excellent dispersibility in water, and good stability. I-CDs were applied in the field of fluorescence sensing, and a blue fluorescence probe was developed for rapid and specific detection of the biomarker 3-nitrotyrosine (3-NT), achieving a detection limit as low as 0.323 μΜ. Furthermore, the practicality of this sensor was successfully verified via the quantitative detection of 3-NT in human urine. Notably, the first CDs-based smartphone-assisted sensing system for 3-NT was established by integrating a paper-based sensor with smartphone RGB color analysis application. The paper-based sensor showed a low LOD of 2.015 × 10−5 mol/L and excellent storage stability. This work provides a convenient and efficient method for on-site detection of 3-NT.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots, Inulin
Fluorescence sensing
3-nitrotyrosine detection
Paper-based sensor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of paper-based fluorescent probes for 3-NT determination
Fluorescence sensor based on I-CDs for detection of 3-NT
Visual detection of I-CDs based on smartphones
Detection of 3-NT in real samples by I-CDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image (Inset: particle size distribution), (b) HRTEM image, (c) XRD image, (d) FT-IR spectra of I-CDs.
(a) XPS full spectrum of I-CDs. High-resolution spectra of (b) C1s, (c) N1s, and (d) O1s peaks.

(a) UV and fluorescence spectra of I-CDs (Inset: I-CDs solution under sunlight (left) and 365 nm UV lamp (right)); (b) CIE coordinate diagram; (c) Fluorescence emission spectra at excitation wavelengths of 340 nm-420 nm; (d) Fluorescence emission spectra at excitation wavelengths of 370 nm-390 nm.
(a) Fluorescence emission spectra of I-CDs in the presence of different concentrations of 3-NT; (b) Stern–Volmer plots for the quenching of I-CDs fluorescence by 3-NT.

(a) The effect of different interfering substances on the emission intensity of I-CDs in the presence (purple) or absence (blue) of 3-NT; (b) The equilibrium time of 3-NT detected by I-CDs. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) The images of different concentrations of 3-NT were added to the I-CDs paper-based sensor under 365 nm UV light irradiation; (b) Linear relationship between 3-NT concentration and the blue value of paper-based sensors; (c) Storage stability of paper-based sensors. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) The UV visible absorption spectrum (black) of 3-NT, the excitation spectrum (blue) and emission spectrum (red) of I-CDs; (b) Fluorescence lifetime fitting curve of I-CDs before and after adding 3-NT; (c) The Zeta potential of I-CDs, 3-NT, and their mixtures at the same pH (pH = 7); (d) Stern-Volmer plots at different temperatures. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Schematic illustration of I-CDs for the synthesis route and the application in 3-NT sensing and RGB color analysis.
Applications of I-CDs in tap water, artificial serum and human urine samples for the determination of 3-NT.
KSV, linear equation and correlation coefficient of Stern-Volmer equation of I-CDs at different temperatures.

head.ce:title: Inulin-based fluorescent carbon dots as effective probes for rapid/visual detection of 3-nitrotyrosine

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Liaoning Provincial Key Laboratory for Green Synthesis and Preparative Chemistry of Advanced Materials (Liaoning University), College of Chemistry, Liaoning University, Shenyang 110036 China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Liaoning Provincial Key Laboratory for Green Synthesis and Preparative Chemistry of Advanced Materials (Liaoning University)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Liaoning Provincial Key Laboratory for Green Synthesis and Preparative Chemistry of Advanced Materials (Liaoning University), College of Chemistry, Liaoning University, Shenyang 110036, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Inulin-based nitrogen-doped carbon dots (I-CDs) with a quantum yield of 28.6 % were synthesized by hydrothermal method. I-CDs exhibited a narrow particle size distribution, excellent dispersibility in water, and good stability. I-CDs were applied in the field of fluorescence sensing, and a blue fluorescence probe was developed for rapid and specific detection of the biomarker 3-nitrotyrosine (3-NT), achieving a detection limit as low as 0.323 μΜ. Furthermore, the practicality of this sensor was successfully verified via the quantitative detection of 3-NT in human urine. Notably, the first CDs-based smartphone-assisted sensing system for 3-NT was established by integrating a paper-based sensor with smartphone RGB color analysis application. The paper-based sensor showed a low LOD of 2.015 × 10−5 mol/L and excellent storage stability. This work provides a convenient and efficient method for on-site detection of 3-NT.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: 3-nitrotyrosine (3-NT) serves as a distinctive biomarker, indicating oxidative damage mediated by peroxynitrite (ONOO−) [1]. Notably, an increase in its level is intimately associated with a diverse array of health conditions, such as rheumatoid arthritis [2], asthma [3], Alzheimer's disease [4], diabetes [5], systemic lupus erythematosus [6], and even myocardial palsy [7]. The normal level of 3-NT in the blood is about 2 nM [8]. The range of 3-NT in the blood of renal failure patient is 10 to 60 μM [9], and that of systemic lupus erythematosus patient is 75 to 117 μM [10] Therefore, precise, efficient, and visible detection of 3-NT is of crucial for the diagnosis, prevention, and treatment of diseases. The existing methods for the determination of 3-NT mainly include electrochemiluminescence [11], high-performance liquid chromatography [12], gas chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry [13,14], liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry [15] and enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay [16]. Although these methods exhibit satisfactory detection performance, they still have drawbacks such as long cycles, erratic electrode signals, tedious sample pretreatment preprocessing, and expensive instruments, which to some extent limit their widespread application. Therefore, developing a rapid, simple, visual, and low-cost 3-NT detection method is a cutting-edge topic.

In recent years, fluorescence detection and sensing strategies have gained widespread application owing to their high sensitivity, simple process, low cost, and ability to detect various substances [17–20]. As emerging fluorescent materials, carbon dots (CDs) have not only adjustable optical properties but also other outstanding characteristics such as photostability, excellent biocompatibility, low toxicity, abundant low-cost sources and excellent water solubility. They have shown broad application prospects in various fields such as biomedicine, catalysis, sensing, biological imaging, photodynamic therapy, and optoelectronic devices [21–26]. In particular, CDs have been successfully demonstrated as versatile nanoprobes for the recognition and detection of ions, small molecules, organic pollutants and biomolecules [27–30]. To date, several fluorescent probes based on CDs have been developed for the detection of 3-NT. For instance, Delnavan et al. synthesized blue-fluorescent Co, N-CDs through a hydrothermal method using citric acid monohydrate and cobalt(II) chloride hexahydrate, achieving selective detection of 3-NT based on an electron transfer mechanism [31]. However, Co is a heavy metal element that poses certain hazards to human health. Jalili et al. prepared molecularly imprinted CDs with high binding affinity for the detection of 3-NT using glucose and concentrated phosphoric acid as the raw materials [32]. Despite the method exhibiting a relatively low detection limit (17 nM), the use of phosphoric acid, a moderately strong and irritant acid, during the preparation process poses a risk of environmental contamination. Developing fluorescent probes based on green CDs to achieve rapid detection of 3-NT is of significant importance. Unfortunately, there have been few related studies.

So far, various carbon precursors have been used to synthesize CDs. Among them, biomass materials have been investigated intensively resulting from their vast availability, low cost, and potential for large-scale synthesis [33,34]. Inulin, as a unique carbohydrate, is abundant and renewable in nature, obtainable through plant extraction and other methods. As a natural biomass, its utilization aligns with the principles of green chemistry, as it generates no harmful chemical pollutants during the synthesis of CDs, making it environmentally friendly. Additionally, inulin, being a carbohydrate within organisms, exhibits excellent biocompatibility. CDs synthesized from inulin inherit this property, rendering them potentially valuable in biomedical and other fields. Both inulin itself and the CDs derived from it demonstrate low toxicity, ensuring safety during application [35]. In summary, the use of inulin as a carbon source for synthesizing CDs offers advantages such as abundance of raw material, environmental friendliness, good biocompatibility, and low toxicity. However, to date, the application of inulin-based CDs in the field of fluorescent sensing has not been reported.

As is well known, the paper-based sensor is a highly promising rapid detection product with merits such as simple preparation, low cost, fine tailorability, good flexibility, and fast analysis. It finds extensive utilization across diverse domains, encompassing analytical and monitoring processes, disease diagnostics, and food safety. There is an independent operating system that might be installed on smartphones, which is easier to obtain and cheaper than some portable laboratory analysis devices. By combining smartphones with visual paper-based sensors, the presence and concentration of target substances could achieve more portable real-time detection. To our knowledge, there have been no reports on the detection of 3-NT using CDs-based paper sensor.

Based on the aforementioned information, blue luminescent CDs were obtained through hydrothermal reaction of inulin and o-phenylenediamine. The fluorescence lifetime and quantum yield (QY) of I-CDs were 8.513 ns and 28.6 %, respectively. Through the photoinduced electron transfer (PET) and dynamic quenching mechanisms, I-CDs were used as a fluorescent sensor for the specific detection of 3-NT and the limit of detection (LOD) was 0.323 μM. Furthermore, a portable sensing platform was devised by integrating a mobile device with the I-CDs-based paper sensor, enabling swift, real-time, and on-site detection of 3-NT. Additionally, the I-CDs demonstrated their effectiveness in quantitatively analyzing 3-NT in urine samples. In comparison to conventional methods, I-CDs and intelligent sensing platforms boast advantages such as straightforward preparation, expedited detection, and heightened sensitivity. They hold immense potential for widespread application in detecting 3-NT across various practical samples, which is of great significance to human health and environmental conservation.
The reagents and instrument models used in the experiment were presented in detail in the supplementary material.

In this experiment, inulin and o-phenylenediamine were used as carbon and nitrogen sources, respectively, and water-soluble fluorescent carbon dots with blue light emission were synthesized by traditional hydrothermal method. 0.2 g of inulin and 0.1 g of o-phenylenediamine and 25 mL of ultrapure water were put into 50 mL polytetrafluoroethylene reactor. After stirring evenly under a magnetic stirrer, the reactor was placed in an oven at 180 °C for 10 h. After cooling to room temperature, the brown supernatant was obtained by centrifugation (12000 rpm, 10 min). The collected supernatant was dialyzed for 48 h using a dialysis membrane with a molecular weight cutoff of 500 (changing the liquid outside the dialysis bag every 6 h) to remove unreacted raw material molecules and other small molecule impurities. Finally, the collected filtrate was freeze-dried at −50℃ for 48 h to obtain purified nitrogen-doped I-CDs powder, which was stored in the refrigerator at 4℃.

According to the reported literature [36], the fluorescence QY of I-CDs was measured by slope comparison method with quinine sulfate solution (0.10 M H2SO4) as a reference and calculated by the following formula:
Φ S = Φ R(Grad S/Grad R)(η 2 S/η R 2).

Here, Φ S and Φ R represented the QY of I-CDs and quinine sulfate (QY is 54 % at 360 nm excitation wavelength) respectively. Grad represented the integral area of the fluorescence emission peak corresponding to a certain gradient absorbance. η was the refractive index of the solvent (η S/η R≈1). To minimize the self-absorption effect, the absorbance of I-CDs and quinine sulfate solution was adjusted to below 0.10.

In the experiment, 15 kinds of test solutions (glutamic acid, K+, Ca2+, Na+, Mg2+, NO3 –, sucrose, phenylalanine, lactose, cysteine, glycine, uric acid, tyrosine, ascorbic acid, 3-NT) with a concentration of 0.01 M were prepared.

A total of 2 mL of I-CDs (0.1 mg mL−1) and 1 mL of one of the test solutions were added to a quartz cuvette, and the emission spectra at the optimal excitation wavelength were recorded for selective experiments. In 2 mL I-CDs (0.1 mg mL−1) solution, one of the test solutions except 3-NT was added, and the emission spectra were measured. Then, the 3-NT solution was added to each mixed solution to observe whether other analytes interfered with the detection of 3-NT. The sensitivity of I-CDs to 3-NT was recorded by fluorescence titration. 1 mL of different concentrations of 3-NT was added to 2 mL (0.1 mg mL−1) I-CDs solution and the emission spectra were measured. In this context, the slit widths for both excitation and emission were set to 5 nm, the photomultiplier tube voltage was selected as 600 V, and the excitation wavelength was set to 376 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of paper-based fluorescent probes for 3-NT determination
ce:section.ce:para: The carriers were pieces of circular ordinary fiber filter paper with diameters of 16 mm cut by a regular punching machine. The paper pieces were immersed in I-CDs aqueous solution (0.1 mg mL−1) for 20 s, and then dried at 40℃ for 4 h, followed by sealed storage in the dark. To quantitatively determine the concentration of 3-NT, a series of 3-NT solutions (1–100 μM) were prepared and 10 μL of each solution was dropped onto the test papers. Then the papers were set under the irradiation of a 365 nm ultraviolet lamp, and meanwhile their colors were recorded using a smartphone. The color parameters, including red (R), green (G), and blue (B) of the photo were rapidly revealed using the Color Desk Application installed on the smartphone. The concentration of 3-NT was calculated according to the linear equation between blue value and 3-NT concentration.

The prepared I-CDs were used to detect 3-NT in water, serum and urine samples. The tap water samples used in the experiment were taken from the laboratory of Liaoning University, and the urine samples were taken from healthy adult women. Serum was purchased through commercial channels. Firstly, the water, serum and urine samples were centrifuged and filtered using a 0.22 μM filter membrane. The feasibility and accuracy of I-CDs detection of 3-NT in actual samples were studied through spiked recovery experiments after diluting the samples with ultrapure water to an appropriate concentration.

The blue-emitting carbon dots were got via a green hydrothermal method (Scheme 1 ). The effects of reaction temperature and reaction time on the fluorescence natures of I-CDs were studied. With the increase in synthesis time, the fluorescence intensity of I-CDs initially rose and then fell, reaching an optimum at a reaction time of 10 h (Fig. S1a, b). As the reaction temperature increased, the fluorescence intensity of I-CDs first went up, and then fell down (Fig. S1c, d). Its peak value was at a reaction temperature of 180℃. Excessive temperature and long synthesis time may cause excessive carbonization of carbon dots, resulting in aggregation and self-quenching, thereby significantly reducing the fluorescence intensity. In addition, the quantum yield and yield of CDs synthesized under different conditions were compared (Table S1). The results showed that under low temperature or short time conditions, the activity of the reactants was limited and the reaction rate was slow, which led to incomplete formation of carbon dots or insufficient carbonization, resulting in low quantum yield and overall yield. As the temperature rose to a moderate range and the reaction time prolonged, the reactivity of the reactants increased, and the reaction rate also accelerated, leading to efficient formation of carbon dots. At this point, both quantum yield and overall yield were typically high, which may have been related to the optimal balance between carbon core formation and skeleton crosslinking. However, as the temperature continued to rise and the reaction time further increased, although the reaction rate increased further, excessively high temperatures or prolonged times may have induced side reactions such as agglomeration and oxidation of the carbon dots, damaging their structure. This not only reduced quantum yield and overall yield but also increased experimental costs and complexity. There existed a certain trade-off relationship between quantum yield and overall yield; therefore, the optimal synthesis conditions (180℃, 10 h) were determined through comprehensive consideration of both factors.

The morphology of I-CDs was revealed by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM). As shown in Fig. 1 a, the prepared I-CDs exhibited excellent dispersibility, with a quasi-spherical shape and uniform size. The particle size distribution ranged from 2.0 to 5.0 nm, with an average particle size of 3.8 nm (inset in Fig. 1a). From the HRTEM image (Fig. 1b), a carbon lattice spacing of 0.21 nm was observed, indicating that I-CDs possessed a (100) graphite lattice structure.

The X-ray powder diffractometer (XRD) pattern of I-CDs revealed a strong and wide diffraction peak at 2θ = 21° (Fig. 1c), which aligned with the (100) diffraction peak of graphite carbon materials. In the Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy of I-CDs (Fig. 1d), the broad peak at 3430 cm−1 corresponded to the O-H and N-H bonds, ensuring the excellent water solubility of I-CDs [37]. The absorption peak at 2814 cm−1 was attributed to the stretching vibration of C-H. The peaks located at 1630 cm−1 and 1356 cm−1 corresponded to C=O and C-N bonds, respectively. These results confirmed the presence of functional groups such as –OH, –NH2, and –COOH on the surface of I-CDs. The infrared spectrum of inulin exhibited peaks near 933 cm−1, 1032 cm−1, and 1051 cm−1, which corresponded to the C-O stretching vibration absorption bands of the C-O-C glycosidic bonds. Notably, the absence of these peaks in the FT-IR spectrum of the purified I-CD confirmed that the final product did not contain any unreacted precursors, further affirmed the successful preparation of the nitrogen-doped I-CDs.

Furthermore, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was used (Fig. 2 a) to reveal the elemental composition of I-CDs. The XPS full spectrum of I-CDs (Fig. 2a) contained three representative peaks at 285 eV, 399 eV, and 532 eV, relating to C 1 s, N 1 s, and O 1 s, respectively. The atomic ratios of C, N, and O were 55.02 %, 8.21 %, and 36.77 %, respectively. In the C 1 s peak-fitting plot (Fig. 2b), the three peaks at 284.78, 286.28, and 287.78 eV represented the C-C, C-O, and C=O groups [38]. The characteristic peaks at 398.68 eV and 399.68 eV in the N 1 s spectrum belonged to graphite N and amino N (Fig. 2c). The three peaks of O 1 s at 531.18, 532.28, and 533.48 eV in Fig. 2d were associated with C=O, C-O-C, and C-O-H [39]. The XPS results were generally consistent with FTIR, indicating that N atoms had been successfully doped into I-CDs, and the surface contained oxygen-containing groups.

As depicted in the inset of Fig. 3 a, I-CDs exhibited excellent water solubility and optical properties in an aqueous solution, maintaining a transparent and yellowish hue under visible light, while exhibiting a bright blue fluorescence under a 365 nm ultraviolet (UV) lamp. A distinct absorption peak was emerged around 269 nm in the UV–Visible spectrum (Fig. 3a), which was attributed to the π → π* transition of aromatic sp2 [40]. Additionally, a prominent peak appeared at 322 nm, which could be ascribed to the n → π* transition of C=O and C=N bonds [41]. The fluorescence spectrum (Fig. 3a) of I-CDs revealed a maximum emission peak at 478 nm under excitation at 376 nm. The CIE chromaticity coordinates (Fig. 3b) corresponded to (0.17421, 0.23019).

As the excitation wavelength increased, the fluorescence emission intensity of I-CDs initially rose and then fell down (Fig. 3c and d), exhibiting a distinct red shift and an optimal fluorescence emission intensity at an excitation wavelength of 376 nm. This indicated that the emission behavior of I-CDs was dependent on the excitation wavelength. Such typical excitation-dependent fluorescence characteristics might be attributed to the uneven particle size or the capture of excited states energy by different surface state [42]. The fluorescence QY in ultrapure water was 28.6 %, suggesting their excellent optical properties.

The excellent fluorescence stability is critical for probes. After continuous exposure to 365 nm UV light for 6 h, the fluorescence intensity of I-CDs remained above 99 % (Fig. S2), indicating their remarkable resistance to photobleaching. In different concentrations of NaCl solutions (0–1 M), the fluorescence intensity of I-CDs decreased by only 10 % of the original intensity (Fig. S3), suggesting that they could maintain good luminescent properties in high ionic concentration environments. The fluorescence intensity of I-CDs remained stable at the pH range of 4.0 to 11.0 (Fig. S4). At basic (above 11) and acidic (below 4) pH values, the fluorescence intensity significantly reduced. There were abundant carboxyl groups on the surface of I-CDs. At acidic condition, the formation of hydrogen bonds led to the aggregation of I-CDs [43]. At basic condition, the deprotonation of –COOH groups resulted in the disruption of the surface functional groups [44]. Therefore, in all subsequent tests, the pH was maintained at 7.0. The fluorescence intensity of the I-CDs solution remained almost unchanged in 0–1 M H2O2 solutions (Fig. S5), demonstrating their excellent antioxidant capability. These results indicated that I-CDs possessed outstanding optical stability in various experimental environments, favoring their application in complex environments and laying a foundation for their detection in real samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence sensor based on I-CDs for detection of 3-NT
ce:section.ce:para: The capability of I-CDs for detecting 3-NT was investigated. With the increasing concentration of 3-NT, the fluorescence emission intensity of I-CDs gradually decreased (Fig. 4 a). When the concentration of 3-NT reached 135 μM, the fluorescence intensity decreased by approximately 96 %. The KSV constant obtained from the Stern–Volmer equation was 6.331 × 104 M (Fig. 4b), with R2 = 0.99782. The calculation formula and method of LOD were described in the supplementary materials and the result was 3.23 × 10−7 M, similar with or even superior to previously reported 3-NT detection method (Table S2), indicating the excellent sensing ability of I-CDs towards 3-NT.

To verify the detection specificity, the fluorescence emission spectra of I-CDs containing different urinary interference (including glutamic acid, K+, Ca2+, Na+, Mg2+, Pb2+, Hg2+, Cu2+, NO3 –, urea, vitamins A, B, and E, glucose, sucrose, phenylalanine, lactose, cysteine, glycine, uric acid, tyrosine, and ascorbic acid) were recorded. As depicted in Fig. 5 a, the presence of other substances had negligible effects on the intensity of the fluorescence emission peak of I-CDs compared to the I-CDs system with 3-NT, showing fine selectivity. When other substances were incorporated into the I-CDs and 3-NT system, there was no significant change in fluorescence intensity, showing good anti-interference ability. Additionally, I-CDs exhibited rapid responsiveness, achieving over 95 % fluorescence quenching within approximately 18 s after the addition of 3-NT, suggesting that I-CDs could be used for real-time detection of 3-NT (Fig. 5b). The outcomes underscored that the fluorescence probe based on I-CDs possessed excellent responsiveness, sensitivity, and a low detection limit for 3-NT, making it a promising new sensor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Visual detection of I-CDs based on smartphones
ce:section.ce:para: The visual detection of 3-NT could significantly simplify the analytical course. After dripping different concentrations (1–100 μM) of the 3-NT solution, the paper-based fluorescent probes showed different colors (Fig. 6 a) under UV lamps. The depth of the color could be seen with the naked eye, while exactly distinguishing subtle color changes on site might be challenging. Utilizing smartphones for transforming color data into RGB values for subsequent analysis presents a practical and indispensable methodology. As shown in Fig. 6b, there existed a linear relationship between 3-NT concentration and blue values, allowing for precise calculations within a linear range of 10–100 μM, with a LOD as low as 2.015 × 10−5 M.

Subsequently, the storage stability of the paper-based sensor was evaluated. As depicted in Fig. 6c, the blue value of the test paper remained identical to that of the newly prepared sample after being stored at room temperature for 7 days. The paper sensor exhibited excellent storage stability, endorsing the developed sensor with immense potential for practical applications. This fluorescence sensor could provide a simple and effective method for portable detection of 3-NT.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of 3-NT in real samples by I-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the practical value of the sensor, diverse concentrations of 3-NT were spiked into tap water and healthy human urine, and the 3-NT concentrations in the real samples were determined using fluorescence spectroscopy. The computation relied on the calibration curve that had been previously derived (Fig. 4d). As shown in Table 1 , the recoveries for 3-NT detection ranged from 93.3 % to 112.1 %, with a relative standard deviation (RSD, n = 3) of less than 4.1 %, demonstrating the reliable performance of I-CDs. The results indicated that I-CDs acted as a selective and environmentally friendly sensor for 3-NT detection, holding promising applications in environmental monitoring.

The fluorescence quenching mechanism of 3-NT on I-CDs was explored by FT-IR spectroscopy, UV–Vis spectroscopy, fluorescence spectroscopy, Zeta potential analysis, fluorescence lifetime decay curves, the Stern-Volmer equation and cyclic voltammetry (CV). The UV–Vis absorption peak of 3-NT at 358 nm partially overlapped with the fluorescence excitation spectrum of I-CDs (Fig. 7 a), which might be the reason for the decrease in fluorescence intensity of I-CDs by the analyte. Hence, it was proposed that the mechanism underlying fluorescence quenching probably originated from fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) or the inner filter effect (IFE).

The occurrence of static quenching typically leads to the formation of a non-fluorescent ground-state complex [45]. As illustrated in Fig. S6, the characteristic peak intensity and position of the FT-IR spectrum of I-CDs remained almost unchanged before and after the addition of 3-NT. Fig. S7 demonstrated that after adding varying concentrations of 3-NT to the I-CDs solution, UV absorption peaks representing 3-NT appeared at 278 nm and 358 nm, and the intensity increased with the 3-NT concentration. The peak position remained unchanged, and no new UV peaks emerged. These results indicated that no new substances were formed during the fluorescence quenching process, thereby excluding the possibility of static quenching. Additionally, after double-exponential curve fitting in Fig. 7b, the mean fluorescence lifetime of I-CDs was 8.513 ns, which decreased to 7.766 ns upon the addition of 3-NT. This significant change in fluorescence lifetime implied that the quenching mechanism was unlikely to be static quenching or IFE, as these processes do not alter the fluorescence lifetime. Therefore, dynamic quenching, PET and FRET were likely the primary causes of fluorescence quenching in I-CDs. For FRET to occur, the distance between the fluorescent group and the quencher must be sufficiently close. The Zeta potentials of I-CDs and 3-NT were −5.39 mV and −10.32 mV, respectively (Fig. 7c). Due to electrostatic repulsion of the same charge, the distance between them was greater than 10 nm. Thus, FRET was denied.

Dynamic quenching encompasses the procedure where excited-state molecules transition back to the ground state through collisions involving energy transfer or charge transfer. During this process, the excited state of the fluorescent material undergoes alteration, whereas its absorption spectrum remains unchanged [46]. Furthermore, in the context of dynamic quenching, an elevation in temperature fosters more effective collisions between molecules, thereby intensifying the energy transfer process and leading to an augmentation in the fluorescence quenching constant. To corroborate the occurrence of dynamic quenching in the process, quenching constants (KSV) at various temperatures (298 K, 318 K, and 338 K) were recorded (Fig. 7d). The KSV was directly proportional to temperature (Table 2 ), indicating that the process was dynamic quenching.

To verify whether fluorescence quenching is attributed to PET, the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) energy levels of the I-CDs were determined and compared with the HOMO and LUMO energy levels of 3-NT. The reduction potential of I-CDs was measured by CV to be −0.59 eV (Fig. S8a). The E HOMO and E LUMO of I-CDs could be calculated by the following formulas:
(1)
E
L U M O
= - e
(
E
r e d
+ 4.4 )
(2)
E
H O M O
= - e
E
o x
+ 4.4
(3)
E
H O M O
=
E
L U M O
-
E g
where E ox and E red represented the oxidation and reduction potential of I-CDs, respectively. Using formula 1, the E LUMO of I-CDs was calculated to be −3.81 eV. However, due to the irreversibility of the redox process, the E HOMO of I-CDs could not be directly obtained from formula 2. Instead, by combining the band gap energy (E g), which could be calculated from the UV–Vis absorption spectrum of I-CDs, the E HOMO of I-CDs was derived using formula 3. Based on Fig. S8b, the E g of I-CDs was calculated to be 3.38 eV, followed by the E HOMO of I-CDs being −7.19 eV. According to the literature [47], the E HOMO and E LUMO of 3-NT were −5.77 eV and −3.24 eV, respectively. As depicted in Fig. S9, upon excitation of the fluorophore (I-CDs), electrons from its HOMO orbital were promoted to the LUMO orbital. Given that the HOMO energy level of 3-NT lay between those of the HOMO and LUMO orbitals of I-CDs, electrons in the HOMO of 3-NT could transition to the HOMO orbital of I-CDs. Following this, the electrons in the LUMO of I-CDs were unable to return to their ground state, leading to fluorescence quenching [48].
Based on the above analysis, it was believed that the fluorescence quenching of I-CDs by 3-NT was primarily attributed to PET and dynamic quenching processes.

Using inulin as the carbon source, N-doped I-CDs fluorescence sensors were successfully obtained via the hydrothermal method. The I-CDs exhibited intense blue fluorescence emission, enabling their application in the detection of the biomarker 3-NT in real samples. Within 18 s, the fluorescence of I-CDs could be quenched by 3-NT through PET and dynamic quenching mechanisms, with a LOD as low as 0.323 μM. To make the detection process more convenient and efficient, the first CDs-based smartphone-assisted sensing system for 3-NT was constructed by combining paper sensors with smartphone RGB color analysis technology. This technology facilitated real-time, on-site, and visual detection of 3-NT levels without the need for any auxiliary equipment. This work provided direction for developing CDs-based optical and smartphone-assisted sensing platforms for 3-NT, while simultaneously expanding the applications of I-CDs in biological and ecological detection.

Yaxuan Chen: Writing – original draft, Validation, Investigation, Data curation. Chen Wang: Conceptualization. Yushi Guo: Conceptualization. Wenxiao Yang: Validation, Formal analysis. Guolin Zhang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition. Qiuhua Wu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Natural Science Foundation for Education Department of Liaoning Province of China (No. LJC201906), Start-up funds for doctoral research of Liaoning University (a280008030) and the Fundamental Research Funds for Public Universities in Liaoning (No. LJKLJ202424).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2025.112750

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
